pages.raw_text:
With no display of fireworks, the
passing of Mayor Moore and in
duction of Mayor Pratt took place
in the council chamber this morn
ing in the presence of several hun
dred citizens and the absence of
any sign of partisan animosity. Re-
firing officers expressed their ap-
probation Of courtesies and reso-
luton of kindly feeling was ten
dered Fred Baldwin, the only re-
firing member of the council, after
IL years service. Mayor Moore
opened the way for his predecessor
in a farewell message.


The appearance of Mr.. Pratt was
vigorously applauded. Mr.. Pratt
read his first message from manu-
script.


To work for the best interests
of the whole city would be ehis sole
aim during his term of ONCE. said
Mayor Pratt, and in this he felt
that he would have the generous
support of the city council. After
referring to the water situation,
Mr.. Pratt said the early constrpc-
ton of the bridges and viaducts in
contemplation was to be sought,
and that the viaducts on Division
and Washington streets should be
built this year.


Work on the new bridge at Olive
avenue was ready to start, he said,
and no delay should occur in clear
ing away the preliminary WORK
leading to the building of the Mon
roe street bridge. The old steel
bridge at Monroe street should be
moved west to Cannon street, he
thought. He suggested a vigorous
fight to combat the epidemic of
scarlet fever and the adoption of
better sanitary regulations to pre-.
vent the recurrence of such cpl
denies in the future. In conclu-
sion, he expressed the hope that
all would work together In har-
mony for greater and better Spo-
kane.


After the approval of President
Lambert's committees the council
adjourned until Tuesday night.
May 25.
